column4: Expert Setup: Maven project is flawlessly configured with JAX-RS (e.g., Jersey). Application subclass correctly uses @ApplicationPath("/api/v1"). Report: analyses JAX-RS lifecycle (Request-scoped vs. Singleton) and provides strategies for synchronising in-memory data.
column5: Strong Setup: Project builds correctly. Report: Accurately explains resource lifecycle and mentions thread-safety for maps/lists, though synchronization details are basic.
column6: Functional: Base API entry point works. Report: Explains the default lifecycle correctly but provides limited detail on the impact of concurrency and race conditions.
column7: Basic: Entry point established but naming is inconsistent. Report: Answer is brief, lacking clear connection between the JAX-RS lifecycle and data integrity.
column8: Non-Functional: Fails to bootstrap the server or activate JAX-RS. Report answer is missing or technically incorrect.

column4: Rich Metadata: GET /api/v1 returns a complete JSON object including versioning, contact info, and resource maps. Report: Exceptional justification of HATEOAS, clearly articulating the benefits of self-documenting APIs over static docs.
column5: Complete: Returns the required JSON metadata. Report: Correctly identifies HATEOAS benefits for client developers.
column6: Sufficient: Discovery endpoint is functional but missing some metadata fields. Report: Basic understanding of hypermedia links in RESTful design.
column7: Partial: Endpoint exists but format is not valid JSON or lacks resource maps. Report: Answer is superficial regarding HATEOAS.
column8: Broken: Discovery endpoint is missing or returns an error. Report question is ignored.

column3: POST a room: Show 201 Created & Location header. GET by new ID.
column4: Clean CRUD: /rooms perfectly handles GET (list), POST (create), and GET {id} (detail). Report: Analysis of ID-only vs. full-object returns, considering bandwidth and payload overhead.
column5: Standard CRUD: All room endpoints are functional. Report: Good understanding of the trade-offs between network bandwidth and client-side processing.
column6: Functional: Room management works, but JSON feedback on POST is minimal. Report: Identifies basic pros/cons of list return formats.
column7: Incomplete: One endpoint (e.g., detail fetch) is missing or buggy. Report: Answer is brief and lacks technical depth.
column8: Fail: Cannot create or retrieve rooms. Report answer demonstrates a lack of understanding.

column3: DELETE a room: Show success, then show 409 Conflict for rooms with sensors.
column4: Robust Integrity: DELETE {id} works perfectly and successfully blocks deletion of rooms with active sensors. Report: Justification of idempotency, explaining the exact server state across multiple identical DELETE calls.
column5: Correct Logic: Business constraint is enforced. Report: Correctly identifies if the operation is idempotent and provides a sound justification.
column6: Functional: Deletion logic is present but fails to return a custom error body. Report: Basic explanation of idempotency provided.
column7: Partial: Room deletion works but the "orphan" check is missing or flawed. Report: Confused or incomplete answer regarding idempotency.
column8: Broken: Room deletion is not implemented or allows data orphans. Report answer is missing.

column3: POST sensor: Show error for non-existent roomId vs success for valid ID.
column4: Solid Validation: POST /sensors validates that the roomId exists before registration. Report: Clearly explains the technical consequences (415 Unsupported Media Type) of content-type mismatches in JAX-RS.
column5: Accurate: Foreign key validation is correct. Report: Accurately describes how JAX-RS handles @Consumes mismatches.
column6: Good: Validation logic is present. Report: Basic understanding of media type enforcement, though may miss the specific JAX-RS exception handling details.
column7: Basic: Sensor registration works but roomId validation is missing. Report: Vague answer regarding @Consumes.
column8: Fail: Sensor registration is broken or ignores JSON payload requirements. Report question is unanswered.

column3: GET /sensors?type=...: Change parameter and show dynamic list update.
column4: Dynamic Search: GET /sensors supports optional @QueryParam filtering by type flawlessly. Report: Insightful contrast between QueryParams and PathParams, justifying why query strings are superior for collection filtering.
column5: Correct Search: Filtering logic works as intended. Report: Clearly explains the difference between path and query parameters for search.
column6: Functional: Filtering works but the implementation is case-sensitive or slightly rigid. Report: Adequate explanation of why query parameters are used.
column7: Minimal: Endpoint exists but filtering logic is incomplete or ignored. Report: Answer lacks comparative analysis between the two designs.
column8: Broken: No search/filter capability. Report answer is missing or incorrect.

column3: Navigate /sensors/{id}/readings. Show nested structure in Postman.
column4: Architectural Mastery: Implements a sub-resource locator for {sensorId}/readings that returns a separate SensorReadingResource class. Report: Detailed discussion on managing complexity and delegation in large APIs.
column5: Correct Pattern: Implementation follows the sub-resource locator pattern correctly. Report: Good explanation of why separate classes help maintainability.
column6: Functional: Pattern is implemented, but class delegation is slightly messy. Report: Basic understanding of resource nesting and architectural benefits.
column7: Partial: Locator is functional but improperly nested or logic is still in the main controller. Report: Superficial answer.
column8: Fail: Sub-resource locator pattern ignored. Report answer is missing.

column3: Navigate /sensors/{id}/readings. Show nested structure in Postman.
column4: Consistent Data: SensorReadingResource supports GET (history) and POST (new reading). POST triggers a flawless update to the parent Sensor's currentValue.
column5: Accurate: Reading management works, and the side effect on the parent sensor is correctly implemented.
column6: Good: Functionality is complete, but side-effect logic (updating parent) is slightly inefficient.
column7: Basic: GET/POST for readings work, but the "currentValue" update on the parent is missing.
column8: Broken: Cannot manage reading history or post new events.

column4: Leak-Proof API: Flawless ExceptionMapper implementations for 409 (Conflict), 422 (Unprocessable Entity), and 403 (Forbidden). All return structured JSON error bodies. Report: Analysis of why 422 is superior to 404 for payload reference issues.
column5: Robust: All three specific mappers work with appropriate status codes and JSON feedback. Report: Accurate semantic justification for using 422.
column6: Functional: Most mappers work, but error messages are generic or one mapper is missing. Report: Basic understanding of 422 vs 404.
column7: Minimal: Error mapping is inconsistent; some scenarios still result in default server pages. Report: Answer is weak.
column8: Fail: No custom exceptions or mappers. Report answer is missing.

column3: Trigger a 500 error: Prove NO stack trace is visible in response.
column4: Security Expert: Catch-all ExceptionMapper<Throwable> returns a clean 500 status. Report: Analysis of cybersecurity risks, detailing how stack traces expose internal paths, library versions, and logic flaws.
column5: Secure: Global mapper is active and intercepts unexpected errors. Report: Correctly identifies the risks of technical information disclosure to attackers.
column6: Functional: Global mapper works but error body is slightly too technical. Report: General understanding of security risks associated with stack traces.
column7: Partial: Mapper exists but fails to intercept all Throwable types. Report: Answer is brief and lacks specifics on "what" an attacker gathers.
column8: Fail: No global mapper; raw stack traces are exposed to consumers. Report answer is missing.
